Day 23 of being reincarnated into this JavaScript world. This time we're digging into Hoisting.
First, let me quickly recap how JavaScript actually runs.
The global execution environment gets created first — that's
The syntax parser starts reading through your code, character by character, like f-u-n-c-t... (parsing it letter by letter).
The compiler turns the code you wrote into instructions the machine can understand.
The "global execution environment" gets created, and this is when
If you want more detail on the syntax parser, I covered it in an earlier post:
[JS Weird World] No.1 — A heads-up on syntax parsers, execution contexts, and lexical environments (article in Mandarin)
So what is Hoisting? Hoisting can be broken down into two main phases:
We already know JavaScript sets up
first in the execution environment. After that, it moves into the two phases of Hoisting. Let me show you with an example. Say this is the code we've written:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

Once JavaScript has read through all of your code, it starts setting up the relevant memory in order. Now, if you paste this code into a system and run it, you'll notice something kind of strange — I'm calling the function
the function is defined, but there's no error. It actually runs just fine ↓

The reason for this is JavaScript's Hoisting behavior. When the code runs, it gets split into two phases. The first phase creates and hoists functions and variables, and the second phase actually runs the code. But here's the key thing to remember:
functions have higher priority than variables, so they get hoisted ahead of variables
. So the code that actually runs looks more like this:

So we can take away one thing here: when JavaScript runs, it goes through the two phases of Hoisting first, and only in the execution phase does it actually assign values to variables or do other work. MDN also has an explanation of Hoisting — here's an excerpt:

Hoisting looks like it simply moves variable and function declarations to the top of the code block, but that's not what's actually happening. The declarations get put into memory during the compile phase, but their actual position in the code stays exactly the same.

So from this we can tell that Hoisting is tied to memory. Before JavaScript runs, it hoists variables and functions to the front and stores them in memory, then later assigns values or handles them accordingly. But it doesn't actually move your code around.
